behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS and REAL-INFRA both apply: recursive watch behavior depends on real filesystem watcher semantics, which in-process mocks often simplify. A mock can hide non-recursive watcher configuration errors.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon as a real subprocess watching a temp root directory. Create `root/sub/a.txt` after startup. Assert exactly one transformed output appears in the output directory for that nested file within timeout, without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS applies at module wiring boundaries (path normalization + config window + dedupe store). Real OS events are not strictly required to catch the core failure, but pure unit tests may miss miswiring.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real event-ingest + dedupe + queue modules together with a fake clock and temp filesystem paths. Submit two requests for the same canonical path inside the dedupe window and assert only one pipeline enqueue occurs; submit again after window expiry and assert a second enqueue occurs.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both apply: duplicate native fs events are OS/watcher-specific. In-process mocks usually emit one idealized event and can miss duplicate-trigger bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon as a subprocess on a temp watched directory. Write one file using an editor-like pattern (create, append, rename/chmod) known to trigger multiple events. Assert the pipeline/output contains exactly one processing result for that file path.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA is decisive: watcher re-subscription after directory deletion/recreation is an OS-level behavior. Mocked watchers commonly hide this failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon subprocess watching a temp directory. Verify baseline processing works. Delete watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file. Assert the new file is processed successfully without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering relies on real filesystem timestamp metadata/granularity and concurrent arrivals. Mocks can hide timestamp resolution and race behavior; OS interaction is part of the failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon subprocess. Create several files near-simultaneously with controlled creation times (or slight stagger to force distinct birthtimes). Capture processing order from logs/output markers and assert it matches ascending creation timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE apply: partial-write timing and fs event delivery are OS-dependent. Mocked dependencies often treat writes as atomic and miss premature processing bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon subprocess. Open a file in watched directory, write first chunk, flush, wait, then write final chunk and close. Assert no processing occurs before close/final write-complete signal, and exactly one processing occurs afterward using full file contents.
